figures.caption: Figure 1: Continuum, band-projected, and implemented views of the Ray–Column representation. Panel A recalls the original orientation-conditioned PRM/IPRM cluster average, in which conditional states are labeled by the direction n = N/|N | and one-point tensors are recovered by the normalized equal-orientation average. Panel B restores the radial coordinate k = |N | and shows the schematic summation over orientation rays and radial packets before band projection. Panel C shows the projection onto finite wavenumber bands, R |n,β
Table 1: Case definitions and initial scale normalizations used in the validation figures. The model normalization is κ0 = 1/2 and q20 = 1.

Figure 2: Slow-strain active-LSE correction. Panels (a,b) compare normalized terminal drain ϵ/ϵ0 for representative difficult strain cases; open circles denote DNS data from Lee and Reynolds [9], dashed gray curves denote the unmodified active-LSE map, and colored curves show the corrected map for the indicated BΨ values. Panels (c,d) show the corresponding raw and corrected LSE structure factors. The unmodified factor χ = 3f : d can collapse in slow strain, while the Ψfd correction regularizes the active LSE structure-to-dissipation map using the anisotropies of the normalized circulicity and dimensionality tensors, fij and dij , and their mutual alignment. The correction applies to active use of the LSE map of Reynolds et al. [3]; legacy IPRM used a modified ϵ equation for the second scale.

Figure 3: Homogeneous-shear validation. Lines denote the reference RC-IPRM closure and open markers denote homogeneous-shear data [8]; component identity is encoded by line style and marker shape. The same closure constants used in the strain cases are retained, and the horizontal axis is restricted to St ≤ 25 to emphasize the approach to the developed regime while keeping the early transient readable. The tensor panels test componentality, dimensionality, and circulicity simultaneously, while panel (d) compares the scalar ratios Sκ/ϵ and P/ϵ. The purpose is to verify that the active-LSE correction and band-aggregate closure preserve the stress and structure evolution while correcting selected scalar behavior.

Figure 4: Elliptic-streamline qualitative guards. Lines denote the reference RC-IPRM closure and open markers denote digitized DNS data [10, 11]. The e2 and e4 cases test whether the closure responds plausibly to elliptic rotation and instability; the high-Reynolds-number calibration role is carried by the strain and shear cases. Panels (a,b) guard against qualitatively wrong energy behavior, such as immediate decay where delayed growth is expected, while panels (c,d) check the stress-structure branch ordering in the adopted elliptic convention. Energy is reported as κ/κ0.

Figure 5: Bardina rotating shear: global energy observable. Lines show global κ/κ0 from the reference Ray–Column closure and open markers show Bardina filtered LES data [7], with color denoting the frame-vorticity ratio ωf/S. The dashed gray curve is a true pure-rotation reference with G = 0 and ωf/α = −1, where α is the imposed pure-rotation rate used for that reference; it is included to separate the pure-rotation behavior from the sheared ωf/S = −1 case. Because the comparison data are filtered LES quantities, this global observable is interpreted as a useful diagnostic. The filtered low-pass observable in Fig. 6 is the more direct use of the retained Ray– Column band structure.

Figure 6: Bardina rotating shear: fixed Ray–Column low-pass observable. Because the Bardina data are filtered LES quantities, the retained band energies can be combined before one-point averaging to form κLP = ∑ β aβκβ. Panel (a) compares this low-pass energy growth with the Bardina filtered LES points [7] using the same low-pass operator for all rotating-shear cases; lines are RC-IPRM low-pass observables and open markers are LES data. Panel (b) shows the effective contribution fractions ηLPβ = aβκβ/ ∑ γ aγκγ for the destabilized ωf/S = 0.5 case, together with the low-pass growth on the right axis. This figure is the main demonstration that retained radial bands carry observable information: they allow filtered observables to be constructed before scale information is lost. The coefficients aβ are projection-specific filter coefficients and are kept separate from universal closure constants.

Figure 7: Lumley invariant-map audit for the normalized structure tensors across the validation histories. The three panels show the deviatoric componentality tensor r̃ij , dimensionality tensor d̃ij , and circulicity tensor f̃ij . Trajectories are grouped by flow family: irrotational strain, homogeneous shear, elliptic-streamline flow, and rotating shear. The plotted states include the main-body validation cases and the irrotational-strain Atlas cases. All sampled states remain within the Lumley realizability domain.
Table 2: Physical constants and representative settings in the current reference closure. Numerical quadrature and band-discretization choices are reported separately from physical closure constants.

Figure 8: Atlas comparison for irrotational strain case AXK. Tensor panels show the diagonal components of r̃ij , d̃ij , and f̃ij ; panel d shows κ/κ0 and ϵ/ϵ0 on the common scalar range used throughout the appendix.

Figure 9: Atlas comparison for irrotational strain case AXL. This case is one of the representative slow-strain examples discussed in Fig. 2; the atlas view adds the corresponding componentality, dimensionality, and circulicity tensors.
Figure 10: Atlas comparison for irrotational strain case EXO. The same reference closure and plotting convention are used as in Fig. 8.

Figure 11: Atlas comparison for irrotational strain case EXP. The common scalar axis in panel d is retained to make the energy and dissipation histories directly comparable with the other strain modes.

Figure 12: Atlas comparison for irrotational strain case PXA. The tensor panels provide the structure-tensor comparison corresponding to the same reference closure and projection density used throughout the strain atlas.

Figure 13: Atlas comparison for irrotational strain case PXD. This case is the second representative slow-strain example discussed in Fig. 2; the atlas view records the full tensor comparison in addition to the scalar histories.
